items.title: Requiring DSS to provide a benefits interview confirmation notice (Cabán; General Welfare Committee)

display.summary: Council is being asked to enact Int 0139-2026-A, which would require the Department of Social Services to deliver a confirmation notice — via ACCESS HRA — to every public-assistance applicant after their benefits eligibility interview. The notice must include a confirmation number, the interview's date/time/location, the benefit type, and 'any other relevant information.' The sponsor's stated rationale is that applicants have been denied benefits for non-completion of interviews they were never properly notified of. If enacted, DSS gains a documentation requirement that creates a paper trail for appeals; if deferred, applicants continue without a standardized written interview confirmation.

constituent_sentiment.summary: Strong modeled lean toward government having a role in ensuring affordable housing access (a proxy for public-benefit access posture): 67.1 citywide on a 0–100 scale; 68.5 in Council District 25.
constituent_sentiment.detail: This is a modeled estimate — not a survey result — of how likely voters are to say that government officials should work to ensure access (here measured via the affordable-housing question, which is the closest dictionary column to 'government should ensure benefits access' sentiment). The curated district-top-issues table that would directly measure safety-net sentiment was not accessible for this run. Both citywide (67.1) and D25 (68.5) scores indicate strong support for an active government role, which aligns directionally with the bill's intent of improving access to existing benefits.
constituent_sentiment.score_direction: toward government having a role in ensuring access to affordable housing (proxy for public-benefit access)

recent_news.headline: Ahead of 2026 FIFA World Cup, NYC Council Passes Package of Legislation… (Council press release also covers Int 139-A benefits interview notice)
recent_news.publication: New York City Council (official press release)

talking_points: The bill's premise — that applicants are being denied benefits for interviews they were never properly notified of — is stated in the sponsor's own quoted rationale. If staff testimony pushes back on the scope of the problem, ask DSS how many denials in the last fiscal year cite 'failure to appear' as the rationale, and how many of those have been overturned on appeal.

The required notice includes 'any other relevant information' — that's a soft phrase. Ask sponsor staff whether that catch-all is intended to capture documents-needed checklists or rescheduling instructions, and whether DSS plans guidance.

ACCESS HRA is the delivery channel mandated by the bill. Applicants without an ACCESS HRA account or stable internet access are not protected by an electronic notice. Ask whether DSS will issue a parallel paper notice for those applicants, and whether the bill or rulemaking will require it.

D25 is one of the most language-diverse council districts in the city. Whether the confirmation notice will be issued in citywide-designated languages is not specified in the matter summary; clarify before voting whether DSS is bound by Local Law 30 language-access requirements for this notice.

Modeled constituent sentiment in both NYC and D25 leans strongly toward an active government role in access (67.1 / 68.5). This is aligned with the bill's direction — a useful frame if any colleague raises 'agency burden' concerns in the chamber.

items.title: 988 task force, DOHMH annual reporting, and multilingual public-education campaign (Lee + Cabán; Mental Health Committee)

display.summary: Council is being asked to enact Int 0741-2026-A, which would (i) establish a DOHMH task force to study NYC's 988 mental-health crisis system and recommend improvements covering counselor training, language access, service-quality tracking, and outreach to underserved communities; (ii) require DOHMH to submit an annual public report on 988 operations (calls/texts/chats answered, average response times, transfers to 911, mobile-crisis referrals, peer-support contacts, follow-up rates); and (iii) require DOHMH to run a public-education campaign on mental health and 988 in English and all designated citywide languages. If enacted, the Council establishes a statutory feedback loop on 988 performance; if deferred, DOHMH retains discretion over both reporting cadence and education spend.

constituent_sentiment.summary: Strong modeled lean toward supporting Medicaid expansion as a proxy for public-mental-health-funding support: 64.5 citywide, 62.0 in Council D25.
constituent_sentiment.detail: This is a modeled estimate — not a survey result — of how likely voters are to say they favor Medicaid expansion. We are using it as a proxy because the curated district-top-issues table that would carry a direct mental-health-services measure was not accessible for this run. Both citywide (64.5) and D25 (62.0) indicate strong support; the gap is below the 10-point district_note threshold.
constituent_sentiment.score_direction: toward supporting Medicaid expansion (proxy for public-mental-health-funding support)

recent_news.headline: Ahead of 2026 FIFA World Cup, NYC Council Passes Package of Legislation… (subhead: 'Council also votes to create new task force to study and make recommendations to improve NYC's 988 system for New Yorkers in mental health crisis')
recent_news.publication: New York City Council (official press release)

talking_points: The bill mandates an annual DOHMH report covering calls/texts/chats answered, average response times, transfers to 911, mobile-crisis referrals, peer-support contacts, and follow-up rates. Use the first report cycle to ask whether 988 referrals into mobile-crisis teams have rebounded to pre-budget-cut levels, and where the gaps are.

Language access is explicitly named in the task force's mandate. D25 is one of the most language-diverse council districts in NYC; ask DOHMH which non-English languages currently have <24/7 988 coverage and what the procurement plan is to close those gaps.

The public-education campaign must run in 'English and all designated citywide languages.' That is a Local Law 30 reference. Confirm with sponsor staff that the implementing rule will name the specific languages in the citywide designation list (currently 10) rather than leaving the count open to interpretation.

The bill creates a task force but does not appropriate dedicated funding for either the campaign or the task force's own work. Ask staff whether the FY27 executive budget includes a line for DOHMH 988 outreach or whether the campaign will be absorbed by an existing communications appropriation.

Modeled support for publicly-funded healthcare (Medicaid-expansion proxy) is strong in both NYC (64.5) and D25 (62.0). If any colleague frames the bill as expanding agency reach without justification, the constituent-direction data backs a posture that public mental-health infrastructure has broad support.

items.title: Installation of illuminated and solar-powered traffic devices at crosswalks (Brooks-Powers; T&I Committee)

display.summary: Council is being asked to enact Int 0013-2026-A, which would mandate DOT to install at least 200 illuminated, solar-powered traffic control devices at crosswalks by December 31, 2031 — with a 40-installation interim milestone by December 31, 2027 — and to submit annual reports plus an efficacy study. If passed, DOT receives a hard floor on installations and a reporting cadence the Council can use to track delivery; if deferred, the agency retains discretion with no statutory installation target.

constituent_sentiment.summary: Modeled lean toward funding more infrastructure: 54.6 citywide on a 0–100 scale; 46.8 in Council District 25.
constituent_sentiment.detail: This is a modeled estimate — not a survey result — of how likely voters are to say they would support increased funding for infrastructure projects like bridges and roads, even if it meant slightly higher taxes. The citywide score of 54.6 indicates a modest majority lean toward supporting more infrastructure spending, while the District 25 score of 46.8 is meaningfully lower (within 10 points so not flagged as district-divergent, but the direction is notable). Solar-powered crosswalks are bond-or-grant-funded capital, not new taxes — the framing in the modeled question is therefore broader than this specific bill.
constituent_sentiment.score_direction: toward funding more infrastructure even if taxes are slightly higher

recent_news.headline: Ahead of 2026 FIFA World Cup, NYC Council Passes Package of Legislation to Prepare New Yorkers and Small Businesses for Opportunities from Global Tournament
recent_news.publication: New York City Council (official press release)

talking_points: The bill sets a hard floor of 200 illuminated solar-powered crosswalk devices by 2031 with a 40-device milestone by 2027. Ask DOT for the geographic distribution plan before that 2027 milestone hits, especially how installations will be prioritized in pedestrian-injury hotspots across the boroughs.

Ask staff whether the 40-installation 2027 interim target represents the worst-injury intersections or just the easiest to retrofit. The packet does not specify selection criteria — your vote ratifies an installation count, not a siting methodology.

Modeled D25 sentiment toward funding more infrastructure (46.8) is meaningfully softer than citywide (54.6). If cost concerns arise, lead with the fact that these are capital-funded devices, not new tax authorizations.

The bill also requires an annual report and an efficacy study. Use the first report as the venue to ask DOT to publish per-intersection injury baselines so the council can later evaluate whether the devices actually reduced pedestrian harm.

raw_context.text: This bill would require the Department of Transportation (DOT) to install illuminated, solar-powered traffic control devices at no fewer than 200 crosswalks by December 31, 2031, with 40 of such installations required by December 31, 2027, subject to limited exceptions. DOT would be required to submit an annual report regarding the installations completed during the preceding calendar year. The bill would also require DOT to study the efficacy of the devices installed and the potential for further installations.

news_articles.publication: New York City Council (official communications office)
news_articles.body_text: that define us.” Providing Confirmation Notices to New Yorkers Regarding Their Benefits Interviews Introduction 139-A , sponsored by Council Member Tiffany Cabán , would require the Department of Social Services (DSS) to provide a confirmation notice electronically via ACCESS HRA to applicants of public assistance regarding their benefits interview. The notice would include a confirmation number, the date, time, and location of the interview, the type of benefit for which the interview was conducted, and any other relevant information. “This important piece of legislation will provide people seeking public benefits with transparency around eligibility interviews,” said Council Member Tiffany Cabán. “Over and over again, we heard from constituents who were denied benefits on the grounds of failure to complete an eligibility interview. This law, which will require DSS to provide written notice detailing the interviews, will help solve this issue and make public benefits more accessible to those who need it.”  Task Force to Study and Make Recommendations to Improve NYC 988 System

claims.claim_text: Int 0139-2026-A would require DSS to provide an electronic confirmation notice via ACCESS HRA to public-assistance applicants regarding their benefits interview, including confirmation number, date, time, location, and benefit type.
source_extracts: This bill would require the Department of Social Services (DSS) to provide confirmation notice electronically via ACCESS HRA to applicants of public assistance regarding their benefits interview. The notice would include a confirmation number, the date, time and location of the interview, the type of benefit for which the interview was conducted, and any other relevant information.

claims.claim_text: Strong modeled lean toward government having a role in ensuring access to affordable housing — by extension, a proxy for support for public benefit programs: 67.1 citywide, 68.5 in District 25.
source_extracts: hs_affordable_housing_gov_has_role city=67.1, D25=68.5; score_high_means='higher score = more likely to hold the stated/positive position'.

claims.claim_text: Council Member Cabán's stated rationale, captured in the May 14 council press release, is that constituents were 'denied benefits on the grounds of failure to complete an eligibility interview' and the bill aims to provide written notice detailing those interviews.
source_extracts: "Over and over again, we heard from constituents who were denied benefits on the grounds of failure to complete an eligibility interview. This law, which will require DSS to provide written notice detailing the interviews, will help solve this issue and make public benefits more accessible to those who need it." — Council Member Tiffany Cabán

claims.claim_text: Int 0741-2026-A would create a DOHMH task force to review NYC's 988 system and recommend improvements covering counselor training, language access, service quality, and outreach to underserved communities; it would also require DOHMH to publish an annual report and run a multilingual public-education campaign.
source_extracts: The bill would create a task force to study the current NYC 988 system and make recommendations for its improvement.

The bill would also require the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH) to submit an annual report on NYC 988 operations, including data on the number of calls, texts, and chats answered, average response times, transfers to 911, mobile crisis team referrals, peer support contacts, and follow-up rates.
In addition, the bill would require DOHMH to create a public education campaign on mental health and NYC 988, to be conducted in English and all designated citywide languages.

claims.claim_text: Strong modeled lean toward supporting Medicaid expansion — used here as a proxy for support for publicly-funded mental-health services: 64.5 citywide, 62.0 in District 25.
source_extracts: hs_medicaid_expansion_support city=64.5, D25=62.0; score_high_means='higher score = more likely to hold the stated/positive position'.

claims.claim_text: The Council's May 14 press release leads with the 988 task force as a featured measure passed alongside the World Cup package.
source_extracts: Council also votes to create new task force to study and make recommendations to improve NYC's 988 system for New Yorkers in mental health crisis.

claims.claim_text: If enacted, Int 0013-2026-A would require DOT to install illuminated, solar-powered traffic control devices at no fewer than 200 crosswalks by December 31, 2031, with 40 of those installations due by December 31, 2027, subject to limited exceptions.
source_extracts: This bill would require the Department of Transportation (DOT) to install illuminated, solar-powered traffic control devices at no fewer than 200 crosswalks by December 31, 2031, with 40 of such installations required by December 31, 2027, subject to limited exceptions.

claims.claim_text: The bill requires DOT to submit an annual report on the previous year's installations and to study the efficacy of the devices and the potential for further installations.
source_extracts: DOT would be required to submit an annual report regarding the installations completed during the preceding calendar year. The bill would also require DOT to study the efficacy of the devices installed and the potential for further installations.

claims.claim_text: Modeled lean toward funding more infrastructure: 54.6 on a 0-100 scale citywide (4,806,073 active voters); 46.8 in Council District 25 (81,836 active voters).
source_extracts: hs_infrastructure_funding_fund_more city=54.6, D25=46.8; score_high_means='higher score = more likely to hold the stated/positive position'.

claims.claim_text: The City Council's May 14, 2026 press release frames Int 0013-A as a measure to install illuminated solar-powered traffic control devices at at least 200 crosswalks by 2031.
source_extracts: Introduction 13-A, sponsored by Council Member Selvena N. Brooks-Powers, would require the Department of Transportation (DOT) to install illuminated, solar-powered traffic control devices at a minimum of 200 crosswalks by December 31, 2031, with 40 installations required by December 31, 2027, subject to limited exceptions.

sources.retrieved_text_or_snapshot: Verbatim summary span from the matter detail page (lblSummary2): "The bill would create a task force to study the current NYC 988 system and make recommendations for its improvement. The task force would be required to submit a report to the Mayor and the Speaker of the Council with their recommendations, including updates to counselor training to ensure culturally competent and trauma-informed responses, strategies to strengthen language access, and methods to track the quality of service that callers receive. The task force would also be required to make recommendations on strategies to address underutilization of 988 among historically underserved communities, as well as outreach and communication strategies targeting those communities. The bill would also require the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene (DOHMH) to submit an annual report on NYC 988 operations, including data on the number of calls, texts, and chats answered, average response times, transfers to 911, mobile crisis team referrals, peer support contacts, and follow-up rates. In addition, the bill would require DOHMH to create a public education campaign on mental health and NYC 988, to be conducted in English and all designated citywide languages." Matter metadata: File # Int 0741-2026; Type Introduction; On Agenda 3/10/2026; Status 'Enacted (Mayor's Desk for Signature)'. Prime sponsor: Linda Lee; 13 council member co-sponsors (incl. Cabán, Louis, Restler, Aldebol, Brewer, Narcisse, Sanchez, Banks, Gennaro, Hanif).
